messages.content: You adjudicate whether found evidence supports, contradicts, or is silent on a market-risk hypothesis. Every stance needs a VERBATIM quote from the result text. Status: 'corroborated' = ≥1 independent source materially supports the mechanism's key premise; 'refuted' = a LOAD-BEARING premise is directly contradicted by a strong source; 'contested' = credible contradiction of a non-load-bearing link; 'speculative' = no signal either way (absence of coverage is NOT refutation). You may revise the likelihood in a bounded way with a rationale; you may NOT change the perturbation or target nodes.

HYPOTHESIS:
{
 "title": "Colo-only apportionment plants the flag in Germany/NL while 35–40% of PDU demand sits in non-colo load with a different country mix",
 "narrative": "Every geo share divides colo MW by colo MW, but rack-PDU demand tracks TOTAL installed IT load — and the non-colo remainder is not distributed like colocation. Germany's installed load (BMWK >2,700MW) exceeds its colo figure by ~1,000MW, Switzerland is self-operation-heavy (installed-basis share 0.13 vs 0.08), and Poland's better-corroborated installed-load figure yields 0.10 vs 0.07. EUDCA confirms the gap is continental: colo+hyperscale is only about two-thirds of Europe's IT power. A venture that builds its Year-1 named-account plan on the colo-derived map will staff Frankfurt and Amsterdam while a third of the actual PDU spend — enterprise and self-operated halls in CH, PL, AT — never appears on its target list.",
 "mechanism": [
  {
   "step": "trigger",
   "text": "EUDCA: commercial colo+hyperscale provides just over two-thirds of Europe's IT power — so roughly a third of PDU-consuming load is outside the colo scope every geo.* ratio is built on",
   "refs": [
    "digest:geo-region-denominator#c11",
    "ledger:geo.DE"
   ]
  },
  {
   "step": "propagation",
   "text": "The colo-vs-installed divergence is country-specific, not uniform: DE's installed-load method gives 0.42 vs colo 0.50, CH's self-operated stock pushes it to ~0.13 vs 0.08, and PL's triangulated 660MW installed load implies ~0.10 vs 0.07 — the €300M gets reallocated across countries, not just rescaled",
   "refs": [
    "ledger:geo.DE",
    "ledger:geo.CH",
    "ledger:geo.PL"
   ]
  },
  {
   "step": "model-impact",
   "text": "The Year-1 plan sells through large operators (colo-centric by construction), so the misallocated map inflates the reachable share of DE/NL and hides demand in CH/PL enterprise halls; the funnel's geography mix shifts against the venture's channel build",
   "refs": [
    "profile:Who is the primary buyer you'll sell through first?",
    "ledger:geo.NL",
    "digest:geo-region-denominator#c14"
   ]
  }
 ],
 "falsifier": "A single-scope, single-source table of total installed IT load for all CE-7 countries (e.g., EUDCA country profiles) whose per-country shares match the current colo-based shares within ±3 percentage points.",
 "likelihood": {
  "value": 0.45,
  "rationale": "The scope gap is documented in the ledger's own crossChecks for four of seven countries and corroborated continentally by EUDCA; the uncertainty is whether non-colo PDU spend per MW is comparable to colo (enterprise refresh cycles are slower, partially offsetting the reallocation)",
  "basis": "evidence"
 }

### Executive Summary

The growth of the digital economy and digitalisa tion is accompanied by a strong increase in the need for digital infrastructure and data centres (DCs). Data centres have thus developed into one of the fastest growing, most energy-intensive sectors in Germany. With over 2,000 data centres and an installed IT power demand of over 2,700 MW, Ger many is already the largest centre for digital infra structure in Europe. The central location, political stability, an electricity supply with above-average reliability and one of the largest internet connec tions in the world combine to make Germany a strategically advantageous place for national and international providers alike. DEAR READERS,

Hardly any situation in our modern everyday lives is con ceivable without digital data – whether we are exchang ing messages on our smartphone, checking public trans port connections in an app or following the day‘s news online on the way to work. But the control of large parts of critical infrastructure – traffic management systems, power plants, heat and electricity grids – also rely on un interrupted data streams.

This data is stored, processed, and transmitted in data centers. In recent decades, these data centers have mostly been located in secret, away from social debate, where they ensured the reliable provision of the services that we all use – directly or indirectly.

Skip to Content

## New State of European Data Centres 2026 report

## State of European Data Centres 2026

The State of European Data Centres 2026 is EUDCA’s flagship annual report, delivering a data-driven overview of Europe’s data centre sector. Developed with national trade associations (NTAs) and leading researchers, the report examines the infrastructure supporting Europe’s digital economy and the forces that will shape its future.

The 2026 edition shows that commercial colocation and hyperscale facilities now provide more than two-thirds of Europe’s IT power, driven by the growing demand for cloud and AI. It explores shifting regional growth patterns across Southern Europe and the Nordics, alongside the impact of new connectivity, renewable energy investment and rising power constraints.

With European IT power demand forecast to grow at a CAGR of 17% through 2031, the report analyses the key challenges, including access to power, cooling innovation, and its growing socio-economic and sustainability contribution. Colocation data centres significantly advanced Europe's 2030 energy, water, and climate goals in 2025 alone, contributing €53 billion to the continent's GDP.

Whether you’re a policymaker, investor, data centre operator, or digital strategist, this report is the critical intelligence you need to navigate the future of digital infrastructure in Europe.

# Inaugural Flagship Report - State of European Data Centres 2025 Click here to download​​

## State of European Data Centres 2025

The State of European Data Centres 2025 is the inaugural flagship report from the European Data Centre Association (EUDCA), offering an unprecedented deep dive into the infrastructure that powers Europe’s digital economy. Developed in collaboration with National Trade Associations and leading researchers, this full report goes far beyond the management summary - presenting a large, data-driven examination of the economic, environmental, and technological landscape of Europe’s colocation and hyperscale data centres.

Inside, the report reveals how €100 billion in investments are reshaping Europe’s data centre landscape, advancing AI, boosting digital sovereignty, and expanding from FLAP-D hubs to fast-growing Nordic and Southern regions. It highlights a projected €83.8 billion GDP impact and over 80,000 jobs by 2030, while addressing key challenges like power access, skills shortages, and sustainability, underscored by the sector’s use of 94% renewable energy.

Whether you’re a policymaker, investor, data centre operator, or digital strategist, this report is the critical intelligence you need to navigate the future of digital infrastructure in Europe.

## European Data Centre Overview

The European Data Center Overview highlights the diversity of the European data center ecosystem, taking into account the added value for our European societies. Additionally, it showcases how data centers operate across Europe. Moreover, the report explores the types of energy data centers use and the sustainability initiatives data centers are implementing.

As a first of its kind, this report aims to create a comprehensive overview of the current European data center market based on the available facts, figures, and examples. Resulting in a knowledge base on the social, environmental, and economic value of this European sector. The European Data Center Overview was created through the fruitful collaboration of several European data center associations. We hope you enjoy reading the report and find it useful for the understanding of the European data center industry.

The Switzerland Data Center Colocation Market was valued at USD 465 million in 2024, and is projected to reach USD 916 million by 2030, rising at a CAGR of 11.96%.

The Swiss data center colocation market is one of the fastest-growing colocation markets in Western Europe. It is a landlocked country; it does not have any submarine cables. However, it is well connected to the global internet infrastructure via underground fibre-optic cables that link Switzerland with neighbouring countries, including Germany, France, Italy, Austria, and others.In Switzerland, the demand for artificial intelligence (AI) is continuously increasing due to the growing use of AI applications across various industries, including healthcare and transportation, among others. We anticipate that this demand will continue to rise in the coming years.
